key_insight: The paper turns short-range-order alloy design into a differentiable optimization problem, enabling direct inverse design from target SRO to target mechanical property.
engineering_relevance: This provides a faster, scalable route for designing multi-principal-element alloys with prescribed stiffness while keeping the generated structures physically realistic.
method: Replace random-swap reverse Monte Carlo with gradient descent on continuous atom occupancies.
Add a physics-based correction term to preserve thermodynamic realism for multi-element alloys.
Use a small neural network to map composition and SRO statistics to mechanical properties, closing the design loop.
evidence_signal: Across nine FCC and BCC alloys, the method reproduced SRO-driven stiffness shifts from -20% to +57%, was 6x faster and 8x more accurate on 4000-atom systems, and matched 3 of 4 Co-Cr-Ni target stiffness values within 6%.
watch_item: The approach depends on sufficiently large simulation cells for reliable SRO/property trends; the paper notes that 108-atom cells can mispredict results and at least 864 atoms are needed for accurate direction and magnitude.
paper_title: Differentiable inverse design of short-range order in high-entropy alloys: from target sro to target property
